title: Chapter XXV.

source.title: NPNF-211. Sulpitius Severus, Vincent of Lerins, John Cassian

verses.book: Dialogue I. Concerning the Virtues of the Monks of the East.
verses.text: “For, if it is worthy of admiration that the flames did not touch that Egyptian of whom you have spoken, Martin also not infrequently proved his power over fire.

verses.book: Dialogue I. Concerning the Virtues of the Monks of the East.
verses.text: If you remind us that the savagery of wild beasts was conquered by, and yielded to, the anchorites, Martin, for his part, was accustomed to keep in check both the fury of wild beasts and the poison of serpents.

verses.book: Dialogue I. Concerning the Virtues of the Monks of the East.
verses.text: But, if you bring forward for comparison him who cured those possessed of unclean spirits, by the authority of his word, or even through the instrumentality of threads from his dress, there are many proofs that Martin was not, even in this respect, inferior.

verses.book: Dialogue I. Concerning the Virtues of the Monks of the East.
verses.text: Nay, should you have recourse to him, who, covered with his own hair instead of a garment, was thought to be visited by angels, with Martin angels were wont to hold daily discourse.

verses.book: Dialogue I. Concerning the Virtues of the Monks of the East.
verses.text: Moreover, he bore so unconquerable a spirit against vanity and boastfulness, that no one more determinedly disdained these vices, and that, although he often, while absent, cured those who were filled with unclean spirits, and issued his commands not only to courtiers or prefects, but also to kings themselves.

verses.book: Dialogue I. Concerning the Virtues of the Monks of the East.
verses.text: This was indeed a very small thing amid his other virtues, but I should wish you to believe that no one ever contended more earnestly than he did against not only vanity, but also the causes and the occasions of vanity.

verses.book: Dialogue I. Concerning the Virtues of the Monks of the East.
verses.text: I shall also mention what is indeed a small point, but should not be passed over, because it is to the credit of a man who, being possessed of the highest power, manifested such a pious desire to show his regard for the blessed Martin.

verses.book: Dialogue I. Concerning the Virtues of the Monks of the East.
verses.text: I remember, then, that Vincentius the prefect, an illustrious man, and one of the most eminent in all Gaul for every kind of virtue, when he had occasion to be in the vicinity of Tours, often begged of Martin that he would allow him to stay with him in the monastery.

verses.book: Dialogue I. Concerning the Virtues of the Monks of the East.
verses.text: In making this request, he brought forward the example of Saint Ambrose, the bishop, who was generally spoken of at that time as being in the habit of entertaining both consuls and prefects.

verses.book: Dialogue I. Concerning the Virtues of the Monks of the East.
verses.text: But Martin, with deeper judgment, refused so to act, lest by so doing some vanity and inflation of spirit might steal upon him.

verses.book: Dialogue I. Concerning the Virtues of the Monks of the East.
verses.text: You, therefore, must acknowledge that there existed in Martin the virtues of all those men whom you have mentioned, but there were not found in all of them the virtues by which Martin was distinguished.”
